skill.name: writing-plans
skill.description: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
skill.summary: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Security review complete. The skill is a planning/guidance template for writing implementation plans. Static findings flagged 16 instances of external_commands showing example terminal commands in documentation - these are FALSE POSITIVES as they are markdown code examples, not actual code execution. Three weak cryptographic algorithm flags are also FALSE POSITIVES - no cryptographic code exists. This skill only generates documentation and does not execute any code.

low_findings.title: Example Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: Skill contains markdown code blocks showing example terminal commands (pytest, git). These are documentation examples, not actual code execution by the skill. No risk to users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain markdown code fences with example commands like 'pytest' and 'git commit'. These are documentation showing users what commands to run, not code that the skill executes.

content.value_statement: Use this skill to generate comprehensive implementation plans with bite-sized tasks before writing code. It structures tasks into 2-5 minute steps with exact file paths, test commands, and commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Generates structured implementation plans with step-by-step tasks
Provides exact file paths for modifications
Includes test commands with expected outputs
Suggests commit messages following conventional commits
Organizes tasks in bite-sized 2-5 minute increments
Documents architecture and tech stack decisions
limitations: Does not execute code or perform actual development tasks
Requires existing specs or requirements as input
Does not integrate with project management tools
Cannot validate if suggested file paths actually exist

use_cases.title: New Feature Planning
use_cases.description: When starting a new feature, create a detailed implementation plan with all tasks, file changes, and test steps before writing any code.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers working on feature development

use_cases.title: Refactoring Projects
use_cases.description: Break down large refactoring efforts into manageable tasks with clear test strategies and commit checkpoints.

use_cases.title: Onboarding New Team Members
use_cases.description: Provide new engineers with comprehensive plans that assume zero context about the codebase.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads onboarding junior or external developers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Plan Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-plans skill. I have requirements for [describe feature]. Create an implementation plan.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have a clear feature requirements and need a structured plan

prompt_templates.title: Detailed Spec Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-plans skill with this spec: [paste spec]. Generate a full implementation plan with test-first approach.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you have detailed specifications and want TDD-focused planning

prompt_templates.title: Quick Task Breakdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-plans skill. We need to implement [feature]. Give me a plan with 2-5 minute tasks.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need quick task breakdown for immediate execution

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Project Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-plans skill. Here are the requirements: [details]. Create a complete implementation plan with architecture overview, file paths, tests, and commit strategy.
prompt_templates.scenario: When you need a complete project plan with all details

output_examples.input: Use writing-plans skill. Create a plan for adding user authentication to our React app.
output: Task 1: Set up authentication context and provider
Task 2: Create login form component with validation
Task 3: Implement JWT token storage and refresh logic
Task 5: Write integration tests for auth flow
Choose execution approach: Subagent-Driven or Parallel Session

output_examples.input: Use writing-plans skill. We need to refactor the data layer to use Repository pattern.
output: Task 3: Update service layer to use Repository
Task 4: Write unit tests for new repositories
Task 5: Run full test suite to verify no regressions

best_practices: Always provide complete code snippets in plan steps, not just descriptions
Use exact file paths with line numbers for modifications
Include both positive and negative test cases in test steps
Break down complex tasks into 2-5 minute atomic steps
anti_patterns: Writing vague steps like 'add validation' without specific code
Skipping test steps or assuming tests will work
Creating large multi-hour tasks instead of bite-sized steps
Forgetting to include commit messages for each task

faq.question: What is the writing-plans skill?
faq.answer: The writing-plans skill is a template that helps AI assistants create detailed implementation plans with step-by-step tasks, file paths, test commands, and commit messages.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use this skill whenever you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task and want to create a structured plan before writing any code.

faq.question: Does this skill write code?
faq.answer: No. The skill creates documentation plans that guide engineers through implementation. Another skill like executing-plans is used to actually write the code.

faq.question: What format are the plans saved in?
faq.answer: Plans are saved as Markdown files in the docs/plans/ directory with the format YYYY-MM-DD-feature-name.md.

faq.question: How small should the tasks be?
faq.answer: Each task should take 2-5 minutes to complete. Examples include: writing one failing test, running tests, implementing minimal code, or making a commit.

faq.question: Can I execute the plan directly?
faq.answer: Yes. After creating the plan, you can choose between subagent-driven execution (fast iteration in current session) or parallel session execution (separate session with checkpoints).
